subject: Physics
q: A positive point charge is released from rest at a distance r0 from a positive line charge with uniform charge density. The speed (v) of the point charge, as a function of instantaneous distance r from line charge, is proportional to
question: A positive point charge is released from rest at a distance r0 from a positive line charge with uniform charge density. The speed (v) of the point charge, as a function of instantaneous distance r from line charge, is proportional to
solution:
12mv2=q∆V
12mv2=q×λ2πε0ln⁡rr0
v2=qλmπε0ln⁡rr0
v=qλmπε0ln⁡rr0

v∝ln⁡rr0

subject: Physics
q:
Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The strings make an angle θ with each other. When suspended in water the angle remains the same. If density of the material of the sphere is 1.5 g/cc, the dielectric constant of water will be ______.

(Take density of water =1 g/cc)
question:
Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The strings make an angle θ with each other. When suspended in water the angle remains the same. If density of the material of the sphere is 1.5 g/cc, the dielectric constant of water will be ______.

(Take density of water =1 g/cc)
solution:
In air:

 tanθ2=Fmg=q24πε0r2mg

In water:

 tanθ2=F'mg'=q24πε0εrr2mgeff

Equate both equations, we get

ε0 g=ε0εrg1-11.5

⇒εr=3

subject: Physics
q: In a uniformly charged sphere of total charge $Q$ and radius $R$, the electric field $E$ is plotted as a function of distance from the centre. The graph which would correspond to the above will be
question: In a uniformly charged sphere of total charge $Q$ and radius $R$, the electric field $E$ is plotted as a function of distance from the centre. The graph which would correspond to the above will be
solution: $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{\text {inside }}=\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{R}^3}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$ $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}_{\text {outside }}\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{Q}}{\mathrm{r}^3}\right) \overrightarrow{\mathrm{r}}$ $\therefore$

subject: Physics
q: A thin spherical conducting shell of radius $\mathrm{R}$ has a charge $\mathrm{q}$. Another charge $\mathrm{Q}$ is placed at the centre of the shell. The electrostatic potential at a point $\mathrm{P}$ a distance $\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$ from the centre of the shell is
question: A thin spherical conducting shell of radius $\mathrm{R}$ has a charge $\mathrm{q}$. Another charge $\mathrm{Q}$ is placed at the centre of the shell. The electrostatic potential at a point $\mathrm{P}$ a distance $\frac{\mathrm{R}}{2}$ from the centre of the shell is
solution:
Potential due to spherical shell, $\mathrm{v}_1=\frac{\mathrm{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{R}}$. Potential difference due to charge at the centre
$\mathrm{V}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{Q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r} ; \mathrm{V}=\mathrm{V}_1+\mathrm{V}_2=\frac{2 \mathrm{Q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{R}}+\frac{\mathrm{q}}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \mathrm{R}}$

subject: Physics
q:
Electric potential at a point P due to a point charge of 5×10-9 C is 50 V. The distance of P from the point charge is:
(Assume, 14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2)
question:
Electric potential at a point P due to a point charge of 5×10-9 C is 50 V. The distance of P from the point charge is:
(Assume, 14πε0=9×109 N m2 C-2)
solution:
The electric potential for a point charge is given by 

V=Q4πε0r

The data given is 

V=50 VQ=5×10-9 C

So, the distance of the point is 

 r=14πε0QV
⇒r=9×109×5×10-950
⇒r=0.9 m=90 cm

subject: Physics
q: A uniform electric field of 10 N C-1 is created between two parallel charged plates (as shown in figure). An electron enters the field symmetrically between the plates with a kinetic energy 0.5 eV. The length of each plate is 10 cm. The angle (θ) of deviation of the path of electron as it comes out of the field is ______(in degree).
question: A uniform electric field of 10 N C-1 is created between two parallel charged plates (as shown in figure). An electron enters the field symmetrically between the plates with a kinetic energy 0.5 eV. The length of each plate is 10 cm. The angle (θ) of deviation of the path of electron as it comes out of the field is ______(in degree).
solution:
Given here, kinetic energy 12e=12mux2⇒ux=em

Acceleration of electron due to electric field is a=eEm, where, e is charge on electron.

Using equation of motion, s=uxt+12axt2  along x direction, we get

L=uxt+0∵ax=0⇒10×10-2=uxt⇒t=10-1ux    ....1

Using equation of motion, vy=uy+ayt along y direction, we get

vy=0+eEmt=eEmt   ...2

From equation 1 and 2, we have

vy=eEm10-1ux

Now, tanθ=vyvx=eEm10-1ux2=e×10×10-1m×em=1

The angle of deviation of the path of electron as it comes out of the field is θ=45°.

subject: Physics
q: Two charges $7 \mu \mathrm{c}$ and $-4 \mu \mathrm{c}$ are placed at $(-7 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0)$ and $(7 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0)$ respectively. Given, $\epsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$, the electrostatic potential energy of the charge configuration is :
question: Two charges $7 \mu \mathrm{c}$ and $-4 \mu \mathrm{c}$ are placed at $(-7 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0)$ and $(7 \mathrm{~cm}, 0,0)$ respectively. Given, $\epsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$, the electrostatic potential energy of the charge configuration is :
solution:
P.E. of two charges
$\begin{aligned}
& \mathrm{u}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{\mathrm{q}_1 \mathrm{q}_2}{\mathrm{r}} \\
& \mathrm{r}=\sqrt{\left(\mathrm{x}_2-\mathrm{x}_1\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{y}_2-\mathrm{y}_1\right)^2+\left(\mathrm{z}_2-\mathrm{z}_1\right)^2} \\
& =14 \mathrm{~cm} \\
& \therefore \mathrm{u}=\frac{9 \times 10^9 \times 7 \times 10^{-6} \times(-4) \times 10^{-6}}{14 \times 10^{-2}} \\
& =-1.8 \mathrm{~J}
\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges $q_1=3\,\mu C$ and $q_2=-4\,\mu C$ are placed at points $(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k})$ and $(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$ respectively. Force on charge $q_2$ is ________ N. $\left(\text{Take } \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = 9\times 10^9 \text{ SI Units}\right)$
question: Two point charges $q_1=3\,\mu C$ and $q_2=-4\,\mu C$ are placed at points $(2\hat{i}+3\hat{j}+3\hat{k})$ and $(\hat{i}+\hat{j}+\hat{k})$ respectively. Force on charge $q_2$ is ________ N. $\left(\text{Take } \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = 9\times 10^9 \text{ SI Units}\right)$
solution:
Position vector of $q_1$, $\vec{r}_1 = 2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}$

Position vector of $q_2$, $\vec{r}_2 = \hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}$

Vector from $q_1$ to $q_2$ is $\vec{r}_{21} = \vec{r}_2 - \vec{r}_1 = (\hat{i} + \hat{j} + \hat{k}) - (2\hat{i} + 3\hat{j} + 3\hat{k}) = -\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} - 2\hat{k}$

Magnitude $|\vec{r}_{21}| = \sqrt{(-1)^2 + (-2)^2 + (-2)^2} = \sqrt{9} = 3$

Force on $q_2$ due to $q_1$ is given by Coulomb's law in vector form:

$\vec{F}_{21} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{q_1 q_2}{|\vec{r}_{21}|^3} \vec{r}_{21}$

Substituting the given values:

$\vec{F}_{21} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^9 \times (3 \times 10^{-6}) \times (-4 \times 10^{-6})}{3^3} (-\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} - 2\hat{k})$

$\vec{F}_{21} = \dfrac{-108 \times 10^{-3}}{27} (-\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} - 2\hat{k})$

$\vec{F}_{21} = -4 \times 10^{-3} (-\hat{i} - 2\hat{j} - 2\hat{k})$

$\vec{F}_{21} = (4\hat{i} + 8\hat{j} + 8\hat{k}) \times 10^{-3} \text{ N}$

Answer: $(4\hat{i}+8\hat{j}+8\hat{k})\times 10^{-3}$

subject: Physics
q: Charge is distributed within a sphere of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> with a volume charge density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>a</mi></math> are constants. If <math><mi>Q</mi></math> is the total charge of this charge distribution, the radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> is:
question: Charge is distributed within a sphere of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> with a volume charge density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>=</mo><mfrac><mrow><mi>A</mi></mrow><mrow><msup><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfrac><msup><mrow><mi>e</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mfrac><mrow><mn>2</mn><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>a</mi></mrow></mfrac></mrow></msup><mo>,</mo></math> where <math><mi>A</mi></math> and <math><mi>a</mi></math> are constants. If <math><mi>Q</mi></math> is the total charge of this charge distribution, the radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> is:
solution:
ρr=Ar2 e-2ra



Charge enclosed between r and r+dr is

dq=ρr 4πr2dr

To get total charge Q

Q=∫0Rdq=∫0RAr2e-2r/a 4πr2dr=-4πAa2[e-2r/a]0R

Q=-4π Aa2e-2R/a-1

R=-ln⁡1-Q24πAaa2=a2ln⁡11-Q2πaA

subject: Physics
q: Two charges of each magnitude 0.01 C and separated by a distance of 0.4 mm constitute an electric dipole. If the dipole is placed in an uniform electric field E→ of 10 dyne·C-1 making 30∘ angle with E→, the magnitude of torque acting on dipole is:
question: Two charges of each magnitude 0.01 C and separated by a distance of 0.4 mm constitute an electric dipole. If the dipole is placed in an uniform electric field E→ of 10 dyne·C-1 making 30∘ angle with E→, the magnitude of torque acting on dipole is:
solution:
The formula to calculate the dipole moment is given by

p⇀=qd⇀   ...1

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (1) to calculate the dipole moment.

p⇀=0.01 C×0.4 mm×1 m103 mm= 4.0×10-6 C·m

The formula to calculate the torque on the dipole in the electric field is given by

|τ→|=|p→×E→|   ...2

Substitute the values of the known parameters into equation (2) to calculate the required torque.

τ⇀= 4.0×10-6 C·m×10 dyne·C-1× 1 N105 dyne×sin30°= 2×10-10 N m

subject: Physics
q: Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The strings make an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with each other. When suspended in a liquid of density $0.8 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$, the angle remains the same. If density of the material of the sphere is $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$, the dielectric constant of the liquid is
question: Two identical charged spheres are suspended by strings of equal lengths. The strings make an angle of $30^{\circ}$ with each other. When suspended in a liquid of density $0.8 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$, the angle remains the same. If density of the material of the sphere is $16 \mathrm{~g} \mathrm{~cm}^{-3}$, the dielectric constant of the liquid is
solution: From F.B.D of sphere, using Lami's theorem $$ \frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{mg}}=\tan \theta $$ when suspended in liquid, as $\theta$ remains same, $\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{F}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{mg}\left(1-\frac{\rho}{\mathrm{d}}\right)}=\tan \theta$ using (i) and (ii) $$ \frac{F}{m g}=\frac{F^{\prime}}{m g\left(1-\frac{\rho}{d}\right)} \text { where, } F^{\prime}=\frac{F}{K} $$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{F}}{\mathrm{mg}}=\frac{\mathrm{F}^{\prime}}{\mathrm{mg} \mathrm{K}\left(1-\frac{\rho}{\mathrm{d}}\right)}$ or $\quad \mathrm{K}=\frac{1}{1-\frac{\rho}{d}}=2$

subject: Physics
q: At the centre of a half ring of radius $\mathrm{R}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ and linear charge density $4 \mathrm{n} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-1}$, the potential is $x \pi \mathrm{V}$. The value of $x$ is _______
question: At the centre of a half ring of radius $\mathrm{R}=10 \mathrm{~cm}$ and linear charge density $4 \mathrm{n} \mathrm{C} \mathrm{m}^{-1}$, the potential is $x \pi \mathrm{V}$. The value of $x$ is _______
solution:
Potential at centre of half ring
$\begin{aligned} & V=\frac{K Q}{R} \\ & V=\frac{K \lambda \pi R}{R} \\ & V=K \lambda \pi \Rightarrow V=9 \times 10^9 \times 4 \times 10^{-9} \pi \\ & V=36 \pi\end{aligned}$

q: The energy stored in the electric field produced by a metal sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. If the sphere contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μC</mi></math> charge, its radius will be: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><msub><mo>∈</mo><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: The energy stored in the electric field produced by a metal sphere is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>5</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi></math>. If the sphere contains <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi>μC</mi></math> charge, its radius will be: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open="[" close="]" separators="|"><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">T</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">k</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">e</mi><mo>:</mo><mfrac><mn>1</mn><mrow><mn>4</mn><mi>π</mi><msub><mo>∈</mo><mn>0</mn></msub></mrow></mfrac><mo>=</mo><mn>9</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>9</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>2</mn></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn></mrow></msup></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
Energy of sphere =Q22C

4.5=16×10-122C

C=16×10-129=4πε0R

R=16×10-129×14πε0

R=16×10-129×14πε0

=9×109×169×10-12

=16×10-3=16 mm

subject: Physics
q:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$ . Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
An insulating solid sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$ has a uniformly positive charge density $\rho$. As a result of this uniform charge distribution there is a finite value of electric potential at the centre of the sphere, at the surface of the sphere and also at a point out side the sphere. The electric potential at infinity is zero.
Statement $1$: When a charge $q$ is taken from the centre to the surface of the sphere, its potential energy changes by $\frac{\mathrm{qp}}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
Statement $2$: The electric field at a distance $r(r < R)$ from the centre of the sphere is $\frac{\rho r}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
question:
This question has statement $1$ and statement $2$ . Of the four choices given after the statements, choose the one that best describes the two statements.
An insulating solid sphere of radius $\mathrm{R}$ has a uniformly positive charge density $\rho$. As a result of this uniform charge distribution there is a finite value of electric potential at the centre of the sphere, at the surface of the sphere and also at a point out side the sphere. The electric potential at infinity is zero.
Statement $1$: When a charge $q$ is taken from the centre to the surface of the sphere, its potential energy changes by $\frac{\mathrm{qp}}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
Statement $2$: The electric field at a distance $r(r < R)$ from the centre of the sphere is $\frac{\rho r}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
options: Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is true, Statement $2$ is not the correct explanation for statement $1$.
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is false
Statement $1$ is false, Statement $2$ is true
Statement $1$ is true, Statement $2$ is the correct explanation for statement $1$
solution:
$\oint \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \overrightarrow{\mathrm{d}} \mathrm{A}=\frac{1}{\varepsilon_0}\left(\rho \times \frac{4}{3} \pi \mathrm{r}^3\right)$
$\mathrm{E}=\frac{\rho \mathrm{r}}{3 \varepsilon_0}$
Statement $2$ is correct
$\Delta \mathrm{PE}=\left(\mathrm{V}_{\text {sur }}-\mathrm{V}_{\text {cent }}\right) \mathrm{q}=-\frac{\mathrm{q}}{6 \varepsilon_0} \rho \mathrm{R}^2$
Statement $1$ is incorrect

subject: Physics
q: If the electric potential at any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in space is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> volt. The electric field at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> will be :
question: If the electric potential at any point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mi>x</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>y</mi><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mi>z</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> in space is given by <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>V</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>3</mn><msup><mi>x</mi><mn>2</mn></msup></math> volt. The electric field at the point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mrow><mn>1</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>,</mo><mo> </mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi></math> will be :
solution:
Electric potential V=3x2

Electric potential and electric field are related as E=-dVdx=-6x.

At the point 1, 0, 3 m, 

⇒E=-6×1=-6 V m-1

Thus, electric field is 6 V m-1 along negative x‐ axis.

subject: Physics
q:
A uniformly charged solid sphere of radius R has potential V0 (measured with respect to ∞ ) on its surface. For this sphere the equipotential surfaces with potential 3V02,5V04,3V04 and V04 have radius R1 , R2, R3 and R4 respectively. Then

Note : This question had two option correct at the time of examination. Proper corrections are made in the question to avoid it.
question:
A uniformly charged solid sphere of radius R has potential V0 (measured with respect to ∞ ) on its surface. For this sphere the equipotential surfaces with potential 3V02,5V04,3V04 and V04 have radius R1 , R2, R3 and R4 respectively. Then

Note : This question had two option correct at the time of examination. Proper corrections are made in the question to avoid it.
solution:
Potential for uniformly charged solid sphere

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q r       outside i.e r > R

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R   on the surface

v = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 r 2 R 2   inside i.e. r < R

Clearly

   3 v 0 2 , 5 v 0 4  are inside potentials ∵ > v 0

3 v 0 4 , v 0 4  are outside potentials ∵ < v 0

To get R 1 : 3 v 0 2 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2

where, v 0 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R

3 2 × 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2

3 2 = 3 2 - 1 2 R 1 2 R 2 ⇒ R 1 = 0

To get R 2 : 5 4 v 0 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

5 4 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

5 4 = 3 2 - 1 2 R 2 2 R 2

1 2 R 2 2 R 2 = 1 4  ⇒ R 2 = R 2

To get R 3 : 3 v 0 4 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3

3 4 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 3

3 4 R = 1 R 3

R 3 = 4 3 R

To get R 4 : v 0 4 = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 4

1 4 × 1 4 π ε 0 Q R = 1 4 π ε 0 Q R 4

R 4 = 4 R

R 4 - R 3 = 4 R - 4 R 3 = 8 R 3 > R 2

R 1 = 0  and R 2 < R 4 - R 3

subject: Physics
q: A body of mass $M$ and charge $q$ is connected to a spring of spring constant $k$. It is oscillating along $\mathrm{x}$-direction about its equilibrium position, taken to be at $x=0$, with an amplitude $A$. An electric field $E$ is applied along the $\mathrm{x}$-direction. Which of the following statements is correct?
question: A body of mass $M$ and charge $q$ is connected to a spring of spring constant $k$. It is oscillating along $\mathrm{x}$-direction about its equilibrium position, taken to be at $x=0$, with an amplitude $A$. An electric field $E$ is applied along the $\mathrm{x}$-direction. Which of the following statements is correct?
options:
The total energy of the system is
$$
\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2+\frac{1}{2} \frac{q^2 E^2}{k}
$$
The new equilibrium position is at a distance: $\frac{2 q E}{k}$ from $x=0$
The new equilibrium position is at a distance: $\frac{q E}{2 k}$ from $\mathrm{x}=0$
The total energy of the system is $\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2-\frac{1}{2} \frac{q^2 E^2}{k}$
solution:
Equilibrium position will shift to point where resultant force $=0$
$$
\mathrm{kx}_{\text {eq }}=\mathrm{qE} \Rightarrow \mathrm{x}_{\text {eq }}=\frac{\mathrm{qE}}{\mathrm{k}}
$$
Total energy $=\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{~m} \omega^2 \mathrm{~A}^2+\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{kx}_{\text {eq }}^2$
Total energy $=\frac{1}{2} m \omega^2 A^2+\frac{1}{2} \frac{q^2 E^2}{k}$

subject: Physics
q: A point charge of $10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ is placed at origin. The work done in moving a point charge $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ from point $A(4,4,2) \mathrm{m}$ to $B(2,2,1) \mathrm{m}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J. $\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{o}}=9 \times 10^{9}\right.$ in SI units $)$
question: A point charge of $10^{-8} \mathrm{C}$ is placed at origin. The work done in moving a point charge $2 \mu \mathrm{C}$ from point $A(4,4,2) \mathrm{m}$ to $B(2,2,1) \mathrm{m}$ is $\_\_\_\_$ J. $\left(\frac{1}{4 \pi \epsilon_{o}}=9 \times 10^{9}\right.$ in SI units $)$
solution:
Distance from origin to A: $r_A = \sqrt{4^2 + 4^2 + 2^2} = 6$ m
Distance from origin to B: $r_B = \sqrt{2^2 + 2^2 + 1^2} = 3$ m
Work done: $W = q_2(V_B - V_A) = q_2 \times \frac{q_1}{4\pi\varepsilon_0}\left(\frac{1}{r_B} - \frac{1}{r_A}\right)$
$= 2 \times 10^{-6} \times 9 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-8} \times \left(\frac{1}{3} - \frac{1}{6}\right)$
$= 18 \times 10^{-5} \times \frac{1}{6} = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ J

subject: Physics
q: An electric dipole is formed by two equal and opposite charges <math><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> with separation <math><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> . The charges have same mass m. It is kept in a uniform electric field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If it is slightly rotated from its equilibrium orientation, then its angular frequency <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is:
question: An electric dipole is formed by two equal and opposite charges <math><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> with separation <math><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi></math> . The charges have same mass m. It is kept in a uniform electric field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi><mo>.</mo></math> If it is slightly rotated from its equilibrium orientation, then its angular frequency <math><mi>ω</mi></math> is:
solution:
τ=pEsin⁡θ
For small angle,
τ=pE θ
α=τI=pEI θ
∵ α =ω2θ Angular frequency =pEI
=qd Emd24+md24
=2qEmd

subject: Physics
q: An electric dipole of moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mtext>p</mtext><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mover accent="true"><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>29</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mtext>C m</mtext></math> at the origin <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0,0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> . The electric field due to this dipole at <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mtext>r</mtext></mrow><mo mathvariant="normal">→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> (note that <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>∙</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> ) is parallel to:
question: An electric dipole of moment <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mover accent="true"><mtext>p</mtext><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>-</mo><mover accent="true"><mi>i</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mi>j</mi><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mover accent="true"><mi>k</mi><mo>^</mo></mover></mrow></mfenced><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>29</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mtext>C m</mtext></math> at the origin <math><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mn>0,0</mn><mo>,</mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced></math> . The electric field due to this dipole at <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mtext>r</mtext></mrow><mo mathvariant="normal">→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>i</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>3</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>j</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover><mo>+</mo><mn>5</mn><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>k</mi></mrow><mo>^</mo></mover></math> (note that <math><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>∙</mo><mover accent="true"><mrow><mi>p</mi></mrow><mo>→</mo></mover><mo>=</mo><mn>0</mn></math> ) is parallel to:
solution:
As r→·p→=0, this means that r→ is perpendicular to p→.

For an electric dipole, electric field at equatorial position is antiparallel to p→.

⇒E→=-λp→,

where, λ is an arbitrary positive constant.

⇒E→=-λ-i^-3j^+2k^×10-29 C m-2

⇒E→=λi^+3j^-2k^×10-29 C m-2

For λ=110-29 

⇒E→=i^+3j^-2k^ C m-2

subject: Physics
q:
Ques:
$\sigma$ is the uniform surface charge density of a thin spherical shell of radius $R$. The electric field at any point on the surface of the spherical shell is :
question:
Ques:
$\sigma$ is the uniform surface charge density of a thin spherical shell of radius $R$. The electric field at any point on the surface of the spherical shell is :
solution: By Gauss law $\int \overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}} \cdot \mathrm{d} \overrightarrow{\mathrm{A}}=\frac{\mathrm{q}_{\mathrm{in}}}{\varepsilon_0}$ $\begin{aligned} & \mathrm{EdA}=\frac{\sigma \times \mathrm{dA}}{\varepsilon_0} \\ & \mathrm{E}=\frac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0} \end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q: Four point charges <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> and <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> are placed on y-axis at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The magnitude of the electric field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></math> at a point on the x-axis at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>,</mo></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>≫</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> will behave as:
question: Four point charges <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mo>+</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> and <math><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">q</mi></math> are placed on y-axis at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo><mi mathvariant="normal"> </mi><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>-</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> and <math><mi mathvariant="normal">y</mi><mo>=</mo><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. The magnitude of the electric field <math><mi mathvariant="normal">E</mi></math> at a point on the x-axis at <math><mi mathvariant="normal">x</mi><mo>=</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>,</mo></math> with <math><mi mathvariant="normal">D</mi><mo>≫</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">d</mi><mo>,</mo></math> will behave as:
solution:
The electric field at P is superposition of the fields due to all four charges. The electric field due to the two +q charges is

E+q=KqD2+d2cos⁡θ1×2
E-q=KqD2+4d2cos⁡θ2×2
The field due to +q is towards right and the field due to -q is towards left. Also θ1≈0 and θ2≈0 .
∴Enet=E+q-E-q≈KqD2+d2-KqD2+4d2
=Kq 3d2D2+d2D2+4d2≈3Kqd2D4
E∝1D4

subject: Physics
q: Two spherical conductor $B$ and $C$ having equal radii and carrying equal charges in them repel each other with a force $F$ when kept apart at some distance. A third spherical conductor having same radius as that of $B$ but uncharged brought in contact with $B$, then brought in contact with $\mathrm{C}$ and finally removed away from both. The new force of repulsion, between $B$ and $C$ is
question: Two spherical conductor $B$ and $C$ having equal radii and carrying equal charges in them repel each other with a force $F$ when kept apart at some distance. A third spherical conductor having same radius as that of $B$ but uncharged brought in contact with $B$, then brought in contact with $\mathrm{C}$ and finally removed away from both. The new force of repulsion, between $B$ and $C$ is
solution: $F^{\prime}=\frac{1}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0} \frac{(q / 2)(3 q / 4)}{d^2}=\frac{3 F}{8}$

If total force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> is exerted on the dipole, find out the amount of positive or negative charge of the dipole.
question: An electric dipole is placed on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>x</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis in proximity to a line charge of linear charge density <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>0</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo>.</mo></math> Line charge is placed on <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi mathvariant="normal">z</mi><mo>-</mo></math>axis and positive and negative charge of dipole is at a distance of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>12</mn><mo> </mo><mi>mm</mi></math> from the origin respectively. If total force of <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi></math> is exerted on the dipole, find out the amount of positive or negative charge of the dipole.
solution:
r=10 mm, x=2,
F→q=2kλr·q
F→-q=2kλr+x·q

F→net=2kλq·xr(r+x)
4=2×9×109×3×10-6×q×2 mm10 mm.12 mm

⇒q=4.44 μC

subject: Physics
q: A cube of side a has point charges +Q located at each of its vertices except at the origin where the charge is -Q. The electric field at the centre of cube is:
question: A cube of side a has point charges +Q located at each of its vertices except at the origin where the charge is -Q. The electric field at the centre of cube is:
solution:
We can replace -Q charge at origin by +Q and -2Q.

Now due to +Q charge at every corner of cube. Electric field at center of cube is zero so now net electric field at center is only due to -2Q charge at origin.

E→=kqr→r3=1-2Qa2x^+y^+z^4πε0a233

E→=-2Qx^+y^+z^33πa2ε0

subject: Physics
q: The electric field due to a short electric dipole at a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> from center of dipole on the equatorial plane varies with distance as :
question: The electric field due to a short electric dipole at a large distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced><mi>r</mi></mfenced></math> from center of dipole on the equatorial plane varies with distance as :
solution:
An electric dipole is a system of two charges of equal and opposite magnitude placed at a short distance.

The dipole moment is, p=2ql.

The electric field due to the dipole is,

E=2kpr3

Clearly, the electric field varies as 1r3.

subject: Physics
q: Force between two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> placed in vacuum at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> apart is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>. Force between them when placed in a medium having dielectric <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>r</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> apart will be:
question: Force between two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>1</mn></msub></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>q</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math> placed in vacuum at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>r</mi></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> apart is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>F</mi></math>. Force between them when placed in a medium having dielectric <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>5</mn></math> at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfrac><mi>r</mi><mn>5</mn></mfrac></math> <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>cm</mi></math> apart will be:
solution:
Using Coulomb's law, we can write:

In air F=14πε0q1q2r2

In medium F'=14πKε0q1q2r'2=14π5ε025q1q2r2=5F

q: Within a spherical charge distribution of charge density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi></math> N equipotential surfaces of potential <math><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> are drawn and have increasing radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If the difference in the radii of the surfaces is constant for all values of <math><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mi> </mi><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi></math> then :
question: Within a spherical charge distribution of charge density <math><mi>ρ</mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi></math> N equipotential surfaces of potential <math><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mn>2</mn><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>+</mo><mi>N</mi><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi><mi> </mi><mfenced separators="|"><mrow><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi><mo>></mo><mn>0</mn></mrow></mfenced><mo>,</mo></math> are drawn and have increasing radii <math><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mi> </mi><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>2</mn></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo><mo>…</mo><msub><mrow><mi>r</mi></mrow><mrow><mi>N</mi></mrow></msub><mo>,</mo></math> respectively. If the difference in the radii of the surfaces is constant for all values of <math><msub><mrow><mi>V</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>0</mn></mrow></msub><mi> </mi><mi>a</mi><mi>n</mi><mi>d</mi><mi> </mi><mo>∆</mo><mi>V</mi></math> then :
solution:
E=− ΔV Δr

∴    △v and △r are constant

E = constant

E=Kqr2=c

⇒   q ∝r2

∴   q=∫0rρ4πr2dr 

⇒   ρ∝1r

subject: Physics
q: Two balls of same mass and carrying equal charge are hung from a fixed support of length $l$. At electrostatic equilibrium, assuming that angles made by each thread is small, the separation, $x$ between the balls is proportional to :
question: Two balls of same mass and carrying equal charge are hung from a fixed support of length $l$. At electrostatic equilibrium, assuming that angles made by each thread is small, the separation, $x$ between the balls is proportional to :
solution: In equilibrium, $\mathrm{F}_{\mathrm{e}}=\mathrm{T} \sin \theta$ $m g=T \cos \theta$ $\tan \theta=\frac{F_e}{m g}=\frac{q^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 x^2 \times m g}$ also $\tan \theta \approx \sin =\frac{x / 2}{\ell}$ Hence, $\frac{x}{2 \ell}=\frac{q^2}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 x^2 \times m g}$ $\Rightarrow x^3=\frac{2 q^2 \ell}{4 \pi \epsilon_0 m g}$ $\therefore x=\left(\frac{q^2 \ell}{2 \pi \epsilon_0 m g}\right)^{1 / 3}$ Therefore $\mathrm{x} \propto \ell^{1 / 3}$

subject: Physics
q: A charged particle $q$ is shot towards another charged particle $Q$ which is fixed, with a speed $v$ it approaches $Q$ upto a closest distance $r$ and then returns. If $q$ were given a speed $2 v$, the closest distances of approach would be
question: A charged particle $q$ is shot towards another charged particle $Q$ which is fixed, with a speed $v$ it approaches $Q$ upto a closest distance $r$ and then returns. If $q$ were given a speed $2 v$, the closest distances of approach would be
solution:
By principle of conservation of energy
Finally, $\frac{1}{2} m(2 v)^2=\frac{K q Q}{r^2}$
Equation (i) $\div$ (ii),
$$
\begin{aligned}
& \frac{1}{4}=\frac{r^{\prime}}{r} \\
& \Rightarrow r^{\prime}=\frac{r}{4} .
\end{aligned}
$$

q:
A point charge causes an electric flux of $-2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-1}$ to pass through a spherical Gaussian surface of 8.0 cm radius, centred on the charge. The value of the point charge is :
(Given $\epsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
question:
A point charge causes an electric flux of $-2 \times 10^4 \mathrm{Nm}^2 \mathrm{C}^{-1}$ to pass through a spherical Gaussian surface of 8.0 cm radius, centred on the charge. The value of the point charge is :
(Given $\epsilon_0=8.85 \times 10^{-12} \mathrm{C}^2 \mathrm{~N}^{-1} \mathrm{~m}^{-2}$ )
solution: $\begin{aligned} & \text { Flux }(\phi)=\frac{\theta_{\text {inc }}}{\epsilon_0} \\ & \begin{aligned} \theta_{\text {inc }} & =\epsilon_0 \phi \\ \quad= & -17.7 \times 10^{-8} \mathrm{C}\end{aligned}\end{aligned}$

subject: Physics
q:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Net dipole moment of a polar linear isotropic dielectric substance is not zero even in the absence of an external electric field.
Reason (R) : In absence of an external electric field, the different permanent dipoles of a polar dielectric substance are oriented in random directions.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
question:
Given below are two statements : one is labelled as Assertion (A) and the other is labelled as Reason (R).
Assertion (A) : Net dipole moment of a polar linear isotropic dielectric substance is not zero even in the absence of an external electric field.
Reason (R) : In absence of an external electric field, the different permanent dipoles of a polar dielectric substance are oriented in random directions.
In the light of the above statements, choose the most appropriate answer from the options given below :
options: (A) is correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not correct
Both $(\mathrm{A})$ and $(\mathrm{R})$ are correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is not the correct explanation of (A)
Both (A) and (R) are correct and (R) is the correct explanation of (A)
(A) is not correct but $(\mathrm{R})$ is correct
solution:
A : Since polar dielectrics are randomly oriental $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{P}}_{\mathrm{net}}=\overrightarrow{0}$.
R : If $\overrightarrow{\mathrm{E}}$ is absent, polar dielectric remain polar \& are randomly oriented.

subject: Physics
q: Graphical variation of electric field due to a uniformly charged insulating solid sphere of radius R, with distance r from the centre O is represented by:
question: Graphical variation of electric field due to a uniformly charged insulating solid sphere of radius R, with distance r from the centre O is represented by:
solution:
The electric field inside a solid insulated sphere is given by 

Einside=ρr3ε0 for r<R where ρ is volume charge density.

Hence, the electric field varies linearly with the distance as

Einside∝ r1

Now, the electric field for r>R is given by 

Eoutside=Q4πε0r2

Eoutside∝1r2

Thus, the graph starts decreasing as a power law graph as shown in the figure for the region r>R.

Hence, this graph is the right option.

subject: Physics
q: Two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> respectively are placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> apart. The electric field at the middle point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> between the charges is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The distance '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>' between the point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
question: Two point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> of magnitude <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>+</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>-</mo><mn>8</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>6</mn></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> respectively are placed at a distance <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math> apart. The electric field at the middle point <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>O</mi></math> between the charges is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>4</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">N</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>. The distance '<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>d</mi></math>' between the point charges <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>A</mi></math> and <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>B</mi></math> is
solution:
Direction of electric field at mid-point due to both charge will be in same direction, which is towards right.

Therefore, E at mid-point =kqd22+kqd22 

⇒6.4×104=2×9×109×8×10-6d2×4

⇒d=3 m
